example_wrong: Actually I live in Madrid (meaning 'nowadays')
example_correct: I actually don't like coffee

example_wrong: The woman was embarrassed and went to the doctor (meaning 'pregnant')
example_correct: I was embarrassed in front of everyone

example_wrong: She is sensible to criticism (meaning 'easily hurt by')
example_correct: That's a sensible decision

example_wrong: I bought this book at the library (meaning 'bookstore')
example_correct: I borrowed this book from the library

example_wrong: We found the exit to success (meaning 'success itself')
example_correct: The exit is on the right

spanish_word_means: caught a cold, has a cold
example_wrong: He is constipated (meaning 'has a cold')
example_correct: He is constipated and needs a doctor

example_wrong: Put the documents in the carpet (meaning 'folder')
example_correct: We have a beautiful carpet in the living room

spanish_word_means: to carry out, to accomplish, to perform
example_wrong: We realized the project successfully (meaning 'carried out')
example_correct: I didn't realize you were here

spanish_word_means: to try to, to attempt, to claim
example_wrong: I pretend to be a doctor (meaning 'I try to be')
example_correct: He pretended to be sick

example_wrong: This fabric produces cars (meaning 'factory')
example_correct: This fabric is made of cotton

spanish_word_means: to attend, to go to (events), to be present
example_wrong: I will assist the conference (meaning 'attend')
example_correct: Can you assist me with this problem?

example_wrong: He has a compromise with the company (meaning 'commitment')
example_correct: We need to compromise on the price

example_wrong: The conductor drove us to the station (meaning 'driver')
example_correct: The conductor led the orchestra beautifully

spanish_word_means: to answer, to respond, to reply
example_wrong: She contested the question from the teacher (meaning 'answered')
example_correct: He decided to contest the decision

example_wrong: I gave him dinner to buy a coffee (meaning 'money')
example_correct: We had dinner at 8 PM

example_wrong: What a disgrace! It rained on my wedding day (meaning 'misfortune')
example_correct: He brought disgrace upon his family

example_wrong: That child is very educated (meaning 'polite')
example_correct: He is an educated man with a degree from Oxford

example_wrong: Don't say such groceries (meaning 'crude words')
example_correct: I shop at the grocery store twice a week

example_wrong: Spanish is a beautiful idiom (meaning 'language')
example_correct: The idiom 'raining cats and dogs' means heavy rain

example_wrong: This is a large street (meaning 'long')
example_correct: We need a large room for the party

spanish_word_means: to bother, to annoy, to disturb, to inconvenience
example_wrong: Don't molest me (en español significa 'don't bother me', no es sexual)
example_correct: Stop molesting children immediately

example_wrong: My parents are coming to visit (meaning 'relatives')
example_correct: My parents are my mother and father

example_wrong: I have a sore pie (meaning 'foot')
example_correct: I baked a delicious pie for dessert

example_wrong: This food has no preservatives (meaning 'condoms' - very wrong context!)
example_correct: This meat has natural preservatives

spanish_word_means: to remember, to recall, to remind
example_wrong: I recorded your name (meaning 'remembered')
example_correct: We recorded the song in the studio

spanish_word_means: to stir, to mix, to shake up
example_wrong: Remove the sauce before serving (meaning 'stir')
example_correct: Please remove your shoes

example_wrong: His renta comes from investments (meaning 'income')
example_correct: I need to pay the rent this month

spanish_word_means: to summarize, to condense, to sum up
example_wrong: Let me resume what I said (meaning 'summarize')
example_correct: Send your resume to the HR department

example_wrong: I need new rope for the winter (meaning 'clothes')
example_correct: I grabbed the rope to climb the mountain

example_wrong: I had chicken soap for lunch (meaning 'soup')
example_correct: I washed my hands with soap

example_wrong: The suceso happened last week (meaning 'event')
example_correct: We celebrated our success with champagne

correct_english: to tolerate, to bear, to endure
spanish_word_means: to tolerate, to bear, to endure, to put up with
example_wrong: I can't support his behavior (meaning 'tolerate')
example_correct: I support your decision completely

example_wrong: He is a very sympathetic person (meaning 'nice, friendly')
example_correct: I felt sympathetic toward her suffering

spanish_word_means: to warn, to caution, to notice, to point out
example_wrong: I advertise you that it's dangerous (meaning 'warn')
example_correct: The company advertised their new product

example_wrong: The argumento of the movie is interesting (meaning 'plot')
example_correct: They had an argument about politics

correct_english: to serve, to help, to attend to
spanish_word_means: to serve, to help, to attend to, to look after
example_wrong: The waiter will attend you soon (meaning 'serve you')
example_correct: Will you attend the meeting tomorrow?

example_wrong: The billón people watched the game (meaning 'trillion' - impossible!)
example_correct: The company earned a billion dollars

example_wrong: The bread is very blando (meaning 'soft')
example_correct: The sauce was bland and needed seasoning

example_wrong: He has a bravo character (meaning 'fierce, angry')
example_correct: She was brave enough to speak up

example_wrong: We spent the weekend in the campo (meaning 'countryside')
example_correct: The children went to camp for the summer

example_wrong: It was casual that we met (meaning 'by chance')
example_correct: Let's wear casual clothes to the party

example_wrong: I studied at the colegio when I was young (meaning 'school')
example_correct: She attended college in Boston

example_wrong: The hotel offers great comodidad (meaning 'comfort')
example_correct: Oil is a valuable commodity on the market

example_wrong: He has a strong complexión (meaning 'build')
example_correct: She has a fair complexion

example_wrong: What a decepción! The movie was boring (meaning 'disappointment')
example_correct: His deception was discovered by the police

correct_english: to sue, to take legal action
spanish_word_means: to sue, to take legal action, to prosecute
example_wrong: The company demandó me to court (meaning 'sued')
example_correct: The workers demand higher wages

example_wrong: We had a discusión about the dinner (meaning 'argument')
example_correct: We had a discussion about the project

correct_english: to have fun, to amuse, to entertain
spanish_word_means: to have fun, to amuse, to entertain
example_wrong: We divertimos at the beach (meaning 'had fun')
example_correct: We had to divert the traffic due to construction

example_wrong: Eventualmente, something might happen (meaning 'possibly')
example_correct: Eventually, we will reach our goal

example_wrong: He is a familiar of mine (meaning 'relative')
example_correct: That song sounds familiar to me

spanish_word_means: to insert, to put in, to introduce
example_wrong: Introduce the USB in the computer (meaning 'insert')
example_correct: Let me introduce you to my friend Maria

spanish_word_means: reading, act of reading, reading material
example_wrong: I have lectura homework (meaning 'reading')
example_correct: The professor gave a lecture on physics

example_wrong: She is mayor than him (meaning 'older')
example_correct: The mayor of the city gave a speech

example_wrong: Many people live in miseria in that region (meaning 'poverty')
example_correct: He was in complete misery after losing everything

example_wrong: Did you hear the noticia? (meaning 'news')
example_correct: I noticed you weren't at the meeting

example_wrong: This is particular property (meaning 'private')
example_correct: Are you interested in any particular style?

example_wrong: Call the policía! (meaning 'police')
example_correct: What is the company's policy on vacation?

example_wrong: Did you see the propaganda for the new phone? (meaning 'advertisement')
example_correct: The government used propaganda to influence citizens

spanish_word_means: to taste, to try, to test
example_wrong: Prueba this food and tell me if it's good (meaning 'taste')
example_correct: You need to prove your innocence

example_wrong: Stay quieto! Don't move! (meaning 'still, don't move')
example_correct: Please be quiet during the movie

example_wrong: He is a rudo man who works construction (meaning 'tough')
example_correct: That was a rude comment

example_wrong: Eat sano food (meaning 'healthy')
example_correct: He needs to prove he is sane

example_wrong: We live in a nice suburbio (meaning 'slum')
example_correct: They moved to the suburbs to raise their family

example_wrong: That's such a tópico thing to say (meaning 'cliché')
example_correct: What is the topic of your presentation?
